title: Efficient Magic State Cultivation for $\sqrt{T}$ Gates
authors: I-Chi Chen, Matheus da Silva Fonseca, Andrew Sornborger
abstract: Recently, experimental and theoretical quantum error correction methodology has seen remarkable breakthroughs. In particular, magic state cultivation has been shown to simplify magic-state preparation and make it feasible for near-term devices. However, recent research on magic state cultivation has focused primarily on the cultivation of $T\left| + \right>_L$. Only a few other magic state cultivation methods beyond $T\left| + \right>_L$ have been investigated. Here, we generalize phase kickback checks for magic states at arbitrary Clifford hierarchy levels in specific codes. We provide an example of cultivation of $\sqrt{T}\left| + \right>_L$ in the doubled color code and the corresponding escape strategy using lattice surgery from the color code to large rotated surface codes. Using state vector simulation for un-grown cultivation, we observe a strong consistence between $S\left| + \right>_L$ and $\sqrt{T}\left| + \right>_L$ cultivation's performance on the doubled color code. Finally, we discuss the application of the corresponding $\sqrt{T}\left| + \right>_L$ cultivation, incorporating the STAR architecture and $T$ gates, for early fault-tolerant quantum computing and its potential to shorten gate synthesis in the fully fault-tolerant quantum computing era.
summary: This paper advances the theory of magic state cultivation by generalizing phase kickback checks beyond previously studied cases. It details a protocol for efficiently cultivating $\sqrt{T}$ magic states on complex codes like the doubled color code using lattice surgery. The results demonstrate improved fault tolerance and provide pathways for realizing early, scalable quantum computation.
main_problem: The research aims to generalize phase kickback checks for magic states at arbitrary Clifford hierarchy levels, specifically focusing on efficient cultivation protocols for $\sqrt{T}$ gates.
main_result: The authors present a protocol for $\sqrt{T}|+angle_L$ magic state cultivation on the doubled color code and demonstrate that end-to-end simulations show high fidelity when employing lattice surgery escape strategies.
method: The methods involve generalizing phase kickback measurements, implementing protocols on specific Quantum Error Correcting Codes (QECCs) like the doubled color code, and utilizing lattice surgery for code expansion.
model_or_system: The work analyzes quantum error correction codes, including the 2D and doubled color codes, which are used to protect logical qubits. The goal is to prepare non-Clifford magic states ($T$ or $\sqrt{T}$) required for universal fault-tolerant quantum computation.
key_observables: Logical error rate (infidelity), success rate, spacetime volume per shot, and the parity outcome of phase kickback measurements.
important_parameters: Code parameters such as $f=3$ ($	ext{[[15, 1, 3]]}$) and $f=5$ ($	ext{[[49, 1, 5]]}$), and noise regimes (with/without idling noise).
main_assumptions: The protocol relies on the ability of the code to support a corresponding transversal phase gate ($P_{n-1}$) during double-check, and simulations often use approximations for computational tractability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate general phase kickback measurement circuits (Fig. 1) and specific $\sqrt{T}|+angle_L$ check circuits on the doubled color code (Fig. 2). Other figures compare end-to-end simulation performance across different escape strategies and noise models.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first generalizing the phase kickback checks, then presenting a detailed protocol for $\sqrt{T}|+angle_L$ cultivation on the doubled color code using lattice surgery. Finally, it discusses incorporating this into larger fault-tolerant architectures like STAR.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to theoretical quantum information scientists working on resource estimation and hardware-agnostic protocols, as it provides concrete, advanced techniques for generating necessary non-Clifford gates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 4. Performance of ungrown f = 3 magic state cultivation using code switching. (With and without idling noise). The success rate (left panel) and the logical error rate (right panel) varying with physical noise strength. The upper panels (a,b) depict results with idling noise. The lower panels (c,d) depict outcomes without idling noise. In the legend, GHZ indicates how many qubit GHZ states are used. Z stab (Z X stab) represents the process with only Z type (both Z and X type, resp.) stabilizer measurements before and after the double phase kickback check. Corresponding shades represent standard errors obtained from sampling.

FIG. 5. End-to-end simulation result for magic state cultivation. The logical infidelity of simulation for f = 3 under the uniform noise model ϵ = 10−3 with idling noise (right plot), and without idling noise (left plot). The curves vary with complementary gap cutoff to postselect the logical error-prone sydrome. LS represents the escape stage using direct lattice surgery between rotated surface code (df = 11) and the [[15, 1, 3]] doubled color code. LS+CS indicates the escape procedure using code switch and lattice surgery between rotated surface code (df = 11) and [[7, 1, 3]] Steane code.
